problemStatement:
A practitioner describes a situation they're stuck on:

"""
It's 2:47 PM and the nightly batch job that usually finishes in twenty minutes has been running for three hours. Priya opens the monitoring dashboard and sees two worker processes, Job A and Job B, both showing "active" but with zero rows processed for the last two hours. CPU usage on both is basically flat.

She pulls up the database session viewer. Job A is waiting on a lock held against the `invoices` table. She checks who's holding that lock — it's Job B. So she goes to look at what Job B is waiting on, expecting it to be almost done and about to release things. Instead, Job B is sitting there waiting on a lock against the `customers` table. She checks who's holding that one.

It's Job A.

She stares at the screen for a second, refreshes the page, thinking maybe it's a stale display. The numbers don't change. Both sessions have been in this exact waiting state since 11:52 AM. Neither one is going to move unless something intervenes — they're each patiently waiting for the other to finish first, and neither ever will. She kills Job B manually, and the whole queue lurches back to life instantly.
"""

What is the canonical NAME of the problem, phenomenon, or effect being described? Reply with the established name, then end with exactly one line:
NAME: <the canonical name>
framework: Coffman conditions (mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait) — break any one. Fixes: global lock ordering, lock timeouts, try-lock with backoff, resource hierarchies, wait-die/wound-wait, deadlock detection via wait-for graphs.

problemStatement:
A practitioner describes a situation they're stuck on:

"""
Maria's overnight batch job for the trading floor's risk report kept missing its 6 AM deadline, and nobody could explain why. The job itself was small—normally finishing in four minutes—but three times that week it dragged past forty minutes.

She started digging through the scheduler logs. The risk report ran as a high-priority process, above almost everything else on the box except the system's core allocation service. That allocation service, in turn, needed to briefly grab a lock on a shared config file—something it did constantly, for maybe ten milliseconds at a time, and normally nobody noticed.

But she found something strange: on the bad nights, a totally unrelated, low-priority nightly cleanup script also touched that same config file lock, and held it far longer than ten milliseconds because it was busy compressing old logs. Once it grabbed the lock, the allocation service would try to acquire it and simply wait. And since the allocation service was blocked, the risk report—despite being far more important—was starved too, sitting idle behind a process that, on paper, should never have been able to delay it at all.

Meanwhile, a dozen medium-priority services kept happily running in between, oblivious that the top-priority job was the one actually stuck.
"""

What is the canonical NAME of the problem, phenomenon, or effect being described? Reply with the established name, then end with exactly one line:
NAME: <the canonical name>
framework: Priority inheritance and priority ceiling protocols (famously the Mars Pathfinder bug fix); raise the lock holder's priority to the highest waiter's.

The moment the key expired, every single one of those requests found nothing in cache and went straight to the database at once, all trying to recompute and repopulate the exact same value simultaneously, burying the database under redundant identical work.

NAME: <the canonical name>
framework: Dogpile prevention: per-key mutex/single-flight recomputation, probabilistic early expiration (XFetch), stale-while-revalidate, and lock-and-refresh patterns.

problemStatement:

She pulled up the network traces. The order service sent a "confirm reservation" request to the warehouse, the warehouse processed it and sent back an "OK, reserved," but that response never made it back to the order service—timeout. So the order service, having no confirmation, retried the reservation. Now the warehouse had two reservations for one order, or sometimes zero, if the retry itself got dropped and nobody noticed.

She tried adding a "please acknowledge my acknowledgment" step, thinking that would close the loop. Her teammate pointed out, half-joking, that now she just had the same problem one layer up: what if the acknowledgment-of-acknowledgment got lost? They stared at the whiteboard, half-drawn arrows going back and forth between two boxes labeled ORDER SERVICE and WAREHOUSE, both people slowly realizing that no matter how many messages they stacked on top of each other, neither side could ever be fully certain the other had truly gotten the last one.
"""

What is the canonical NAME of the problem, phenomenon, or effect being described? Reply with the established name, then end with exactly one line:
NAME: <the canonical name>
framework: Impossibility of guaranteed consensus over a lossy channel; accept it and design around it with idempotency, at-least-once + dedup, and eventual reconciliation rather than perfect agreement.

NAME: <the canonical name>
framework: Reduce hold time and lock frequency; use adaptive/spin-then-block locks, lock-free structures, backoff, or fair-but-batched handoff to break the convoy.

problemStatement:

On-call at the hospital's staffing system, Priya got paged about a compliance violation: two doctors, Chen and Okafor, had both gone off-call for the same shift, leaving zero doctors on duty. That shouldn't have been possible—the app enforced a rule that at least one doctor had to remain on-call before anyone could drop out.

She pulled the logs. At 11:58pm, Chen opened the app, saw Okafor still listed as on-call, and figured it was safe to sign off. At 11:58:03, Okafor opened the app on his phone, saw Chen still listed as on-call, and signed off too. Both checks passed. Both updates committed. Neither doctor ever saw the other's change before making their own decision, because each read the "current" state a few seconds before the other's write landed.

Priya stared at the timestamps, three seconds apart, and realized the system had never actually stopped two people from removing the last two constraints on a shared invariant at the same time—it only checked one row at a time, from each person's own point of view, without knowing what the other was about to do simultaneously.

NAME: <the canonical name>
framework: Snapshot isolation's blind spot; fix with Serializable Snapshot Isolation, SELECT ... FOR UPDATE / materializing the conflict, or promoting to true serializable isolation.

problemStatement:

She pulled up the logs from the checkout service and the ledger service side by side. The checkout service stamped an order at 14:32:07, but by the time it hit the ledger service, the timestamp read 14:31:58 — nine seconds *earlier* than when it was supposedly created. That shouldn't be possible.

She checked the actual sequence of events using request IDs and traced them through the message queue. The order genuinely was created first, then processed downstream, yet the downstream timestamp claimed it happened before the upstream one. The sequence numbers were correct — everything arrived and processed in the right order. Only the wall-clock timestamps were lying to her.

She SSH'd into both containers and ran `date` on each. The checkout host said 14:47:03. The ledger host said 14:46:44. Nineteen seconds apart, and drifting further each time she checked. Nothing in her code was wrong — the two machines themselves simply disagreed about what time it currently was.
"""

What is the canonical NAME of the problem, phenomenon, or effect being described? Reply with the established name, then end with exactly one line:
NAME: <the canonical name>
framework: Don't trust wall clocks for ordering: use logical clocks (Lamport), vector clocks, hybrid logical clocks, or bounded-uncertainty clocks (Spanner TrueTime with commit-wait).

problemStatement:

Then marketing asked her to incorporate more signals: time on site, click patterns across twelve categories, seasonal timing, referral source, age bracket, past return rate, newsletter engagement, and a dozen other behavioral flags. Thirty-some features in total. Confident that more data meant better matches, she rebuilt the model.

The results were bizarre. Customers who seemed obviously similar—same budget, same style, same room size—now showed up as distant neighbors, while people with wildly different profiles clustered together. She checked the distance calculations obsessively, certain there was a bug. There wasn't. When she printed out the actual distance values, something strange emerged: almost everyone was roughly the same distance from everyone else. The nearest neighbor and the farthest neighbor differed by only a few percent. It was as if the space had flattened out, erasing any meaningful notion of "close" or "far," no matter how she normalized or scaled the inputs.
"""

What is the canonical NAME of the problem, phenomenon, or effect being described? Reply with the established name, then end with exactly one line:
NAME: <the canonical name>
framework: In high dimensions, distance concentration and exponential volume growth break intuition. Unlocks dimensionality reduction (PCA, random projections), feature selection, metric learning, and approximate nearest-neighbor (LSH) instead of exact geometry.

problemStatement:

NAME: <the canonical name>
framework: Speedup is capped at 1/(s + (1−s)/N) by the serial fraction s. Redirects effort to shrinking the serial portion or the sync/communication overhead, and pairs with Gustafson's Law when you can scale the problem size instead.

problemStatement:

She starts mapping out exactly who gets admitted versus sent home. A patient with diabetes showing even mild pneumonia symptoms gets admitted immediately as a precaution. A patient without diabetes has to look considerably sicker before doctors decide to admit them. NAME: <the canonical name>
framework: Conditioning on a common effect (a collider) of two variables induces a spurious association between them; the DAG-based fix is to avoid selecting or stratifying on that collider, or to model the selection mechanism.

problemStatement:
